title: Charmed Life - Chapter 13 (Fragment 4)
text:
вАЬYou poor darlings! Another time you get unhappy, you must come and tell me at once ! And what a thing too! I was so afraid youвАЩd got into real trouble, and then Julia told me it was her. IвАЩm extremely vexed with her. A girl did that to me once and I know how miserable it made me. Now, please, please come back. IвАЩve got a surprise waiting for you at the Castle.вАЭ

There was nothing Cat and Janet could do but climb into the back of the car and be driven back to the Castle. They were miserable. CatвАЩs misery was increased by the fact that he began to feel sick from the moment Millie started to bump the car backwards down the lane to a gate where she could turn it. The smell of blackberry coming from JanetвАЩs squashy hat made him feel worse.

Millie, Roger, and Julia were very relieved to have found them. They chattered joyfully the whole way. Through his sickness, Cat got the impression that, although none of them said so, what they were particularly glad about was to have found Janet and Cat before Chrestomanci came to hear they were gone. This did not make either Cat or Janet feel any better.

In five minutes, the car had whispered up the avenue and stopped at the main door of the Castle. The butler opened it for them just, Cat thought sadly, as Gwendolen would have wished. The butler, furthermore, ceremoniously took JanetвАЩs leaking hat away from her. вАЬIвАЩll see that these get to Cook,вАЭ he said.

Millie told Janet that her dress would just pass muster and hurried them to what was called the Little Drawing Room. вАЬWhich means, of course, that itвАЩs a mere seventy feet square,вАЭ she said. вАЬGo in. Tea will be there for you.вАЭ

They went in. In the middle of the big, square room, a wispy, skinny woman in beaded black clothes was sitting nervously on the edge of a gilded chair. She jumped around when the door opened.

Cat forgot he felt sick. вАЬMrs. Sharp!вАЭ he shouted, and ran to hug her.

Mrs. Sharp was overjoyed, in spite of her nervousness. вАЬItвАЩs my Cat, then! Here, stand back, let me look at you, and you too, Gwendolen, love. My word, you do wear fine clothes to go playing about in! YouвАЩre fatter, Cat. And Gwendolen, youвАЩve gone thin. I can understand that, dear, believe me! And would you just look at the tea theyвАЩve brought for the three of us!вАЭ

It was a marvelous tea, even better than the tea on the lawn. Mrs. Sharp, in her old greedy way, settled down to eat as much as she could, and to gossip hard. вАЬYes, we came up on the train yesterday, Mr. Nostrum and me. After I got your postcard, Cat, I couldnвАЩt rest till IвАЩd had a look at you both, and seeing as how my contacts and other things have been paying nicely, I felt I owed it to myself. They treated me like royalty when I turned up here at the door too. I canвАЩt fault them. But I wish I cared for it in this Castle. Tell me, Gwendolen, love, does it get you like it gets me?вАЭ

вАЬHow does it get you?вАЭ Janet asked cautiously.

вАЬIвАЩm nerves all over,вАЭ said Mrs. Sharp. вАЬI feel weak and jumpy as a kittenвАФand that reminds me, Cat, but IвАЩll tell you later. ItвАЩs so quiet here. I kept trying to think what it was before you cameвАФand you were a long time, my lovesвАФand at last it came to me. ItвАЩs an enchantment, thatвАЩs what it is, a terrible strong one too, against us witches. I said, вАШThis Castle does not love witches, thatвАЩs what it is!вАЩ and I felt for you, Gwendolen. Make him send you to school away somewhere. YouвАЩd be happier.вАЭ

She chattered on. She was delighted to see them both, and she kept giving Cat particularly proud and affectionate looks. Cat thought she had convinced herself she had brought him up from a baby. After all, she had known him since he was born.

vocabulary.definition: In a formal or dignified manner, often with emphasis on tradition or ritual.
vocabulary.example: The butler ceremoniously took JanetвАЩs leaking hat away from her.

vocabulary.definition: To talk rapidly or incessantly about trivial matters.
vocabulary.example: They chattered joyfully the whole way.

vocabulary.definition: People who can provide information, support, or help, especially in a professional context.
vocabulary.example: My contacts and other things have been paying nicely.

vocabulary.definition: A room where guests are entertained, often synonymous with drawing room.
vocabulary.example: The Little Drawing Room where they hurried.

vocabulary.definition: A room in a house where guests may be entertained.
vocabulary.example: They hurried to the Little Drawing Room.

vocabulary.definition: A magical spell or charm.
vocabulary.example: ItвАЩs an enchantment, a terrible strong one too, against us witches.

vocabulary.definition: Having more fat than before or than another person or thing.
vocabulary.example: YouвАЩre fatter, Cat.

vocabulary.definition: Covered thinly with gold leaf or gold paint.
vocabulary.example: She was sitting nervously on the edge of a gilded chair.

vocabulary.definition: Casual or unconstrained conversation or reports about other people, typically involving details that are not confirmed as true.
vocabulary.example: Mrs. Sharp settled down to eat and gossip hard.

vocabulary.definition: Having an intense and selfish desire for something, especially wealth or food.
vocabulary.example: Mrs. Sharp, in her old greedy way.

vocabulary.definition: An idea, feeling, or opinion about something or someone formed without conscious thought.
vocabulary.example: Cat got the impression that they were glad to have found Janet and Cat.

vocabulary.definition: To move quickly and energetically in different directions, often because of excitement or nervousness.
vocabulary.example: She jumped around when the door opened.

vocabulary.definition: Allowing liquid or gas to escape through a hole or crack.
vocabulary.example: The butler took JanetвАЩs leaking hat away from her.

vocabulary.definition: Very unhappy or uncomfortable.
vocabulary.example: They were miserable.

vocabulary.definition: An assemblage or gathering, or the quality required to meet a standard.
vocabulary.example: Her dress would just pass muster.

vocabulary.definition: The state of being anxious or worried.
vocabulary.example: She was sitting nervously on the edge of a gilded chair.

vocabulary.definition: No longer feeling anxious or distressed.
vocabulary.example: Millie, Roger, and Julia were very relieved to have found them.

vocabulary.definition: Members of a royal family; formal honour or treatment as if from a royal person.
vocabulary.example: They treated me like royalty when I turned up here.

vocabulary.definition: Very thin, sometimes in an unattractive way.
vocabulary.example: A wispy, skinny woman in beaded black clothes.

vocabulary.definition: Soft and easily crushed or flattened.
vocabulary.example: The smell of blackberry coming from JanetвАЩs squashy hat.

vocabulary.definition: An unexpected event or fact that causes wonder or astonishment.
vocabulary.example: IвАЩve got a surprise waiting for you at the Castle.

vocabulary.definition: Annoyed, frustrated, or worried.
vocabulary.example: IвАЩm extremely vexed with her.

vocabulary.definition: Fine, thin, or slight in appearance.
vocabulary.example: A wispy, skinny woman in beaded black clothes.

vocabulary.definition: A woman thought to have magic powers, especially evil ones.
vocabulary.example: This Castle does not love witches.
